kids.question: Who wrote the letter to the Romans?
kids.option_a: Paul
kids.explanation: Romans 1:1 — "Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ, called to be an apostle, separated unto the gospel of God."

kids.question: What did Paul say he was not ashamed of?
kids.option_a: The gospel of Christ
kids.explanation: Romans 1:16 — "For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ: for it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth."

kids.question: Can people see that God exists by looking at creation?
kids.option_a: Yes, God's power and nature are clearly seen in what He made
kids.option_b: No, there is no evidence in nature
kids.explanation: Romans 1:20 — "For the invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made."

teens.question: What is the gospel the power of God unto?
teens.option_a: Salvation to every one that believeth — to the Jew first, and also to the Greek
teens.explanation: Romans 1:16 — "For it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth; to the Jew first, and also to the Greek."

teens.question: What is revealed from faith to faith in the gospel?
teens.option_a: The righteousness of God
teens.explanation: Romans 1:17 — "For therein is the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith: as it is written, The just shall live by faith."

teens.question: What is revealed from heaven against ungodliness?
teens.option_a: The wrath of God
teens.explanation: Romans 1:18 — "For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men, who hold the truth in unrighteousness."

teens.question: What did people do even though they knew God?
teens.option_a: They glorified Him not as God, neither were thankful, but became vain in their imaginations
teens.option_c: They built temples in His honour
teens.option_d: They spread the truth to all nations
teens.explanation: Romans 1:21 — "Because that, when they knew God, they glorified him not as God, neither were thankful; but became vain in their imaginations."

teens.question: What did people exchange the glory of God for?
teens.option_a: Images made like corruptible man, birds, fourfooted beasts, and creeping things
teens.explanation: Romans 1:23 — "And changed the glory of the uncorruptible God into an image made like to corruptible man, and to birds, and fourfooted beasts, and creeping things."

adults.question: How does Paul describe himself in the opening verse?
adults.option_a: A servant of Jesus Christ, called to be an apostle, separated unto the gospel of God
adults.option_b: A Pharisee and teacher of the law
adults.option_c: A Roman citizen with full rights
adults.explanation: Romans 1:1 — "Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ, called to be an apostle, separated unto the gospel of God."

adults.question: What does Paul say about Jesus Christ according to the flesh and according to the spirit of holiness?
adults.option_a: Made of the seed of David according to the flesh, and declared to be the Son of God with power according to the spirit of holiness by the resurrection from the dead
adults.option_b: Born of Roman lineage and adopted by God
adults.option_c: Created as an angel and later made human
adults.option_d: A prophet from the tribe of Levi
adults.explanation: Romans 1:3-4 — "Made of the seed of David according to the flesh; And declared to be the Son of God with power, according to the spirit of holiness, by the resurrection from the dead."

adults.question: What Old Testament quotation does Paul cite in verse 17?
adults.option_a: "The just shall live by faith" — from Habakkuk 2:4
adults.option_b: "The Lord is my shepherd" — from Psalm 23
adults.option_c: "In the beginning God created" — from Genesis 1
adults.option_d: "Thou shalt love the Lord thy God" — from Deuteronomy 6
adults.explanation: Romans 1:17 — "As it is written, The just shall live by faith" (Habakkuk 2:4). This verse became the rallying cry of the Reformation.

adults.question: What does Paul say people are "without excuse" for?
adults.option_a: Not acknowledging God — because His invisible attributes are clearly seen through creation
adults.explanation: Romans 1:20 — "For the invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen... so that they are without excuse."

adults.question: What does "God gave them up" (repeated three times) indicate about divine judgment?
adults.option_a: God judicially handed them over to the consequences of their sinful choices — a form of judgment through abandonment
adults.option_b: God physically removed them from earth
adults.explanation: Romans 1:24,26,28 — "God gave them up" appears three times, showing progressive judicial abandonment: to uncleanness (v.24), vile affections (v.26), and a reprobate mind (v.28).

adults.question: What did people exchange the truth of God for?
adults.option_a: A lie — and worshipped and served the creature more than the Creator
adults.explanation: Romans 1:25 — "Who changed the truth of God into a lie, and worshipped and served the creature more than the Creator, who is blessed for ever. Amen."

adults.question: What does Paul mean by a "reprobate mind" in verse 28?
adults.option_a: A mind that God has given over to moral corruption because people rejected the knowledge of God — unable to discern right from wrong
adults.explanation: Romans 1:28 — "And even as they did not like to retain God in their knowledge, God gave them over to a reprobate mind, to do those things which are not convenient."

adults.question: What does Paul say about those who approve of sinful behaviour in verse 32?
adults.option_a: They not only do the same but have pleasure in them that do them — knowing the judgment of God that such things are worthy of death
adults.explanation: Romans 1:32 — "Who knowing the judgment of God, that they which commit such things are worthy of death, not only do the same, but have pleasure in them that do them."

kids.question: Does God show favouritism between people?
kids.option_a: No, there is no respect of persons with God
kids.option_b: Yes, He only loves Jewish people
kids.option_c: Yes, He only loves rich people
kids.option_d: Yes, He only loves strong people
kids.explanation: Romans 2:11 — "For there is no respect of persons with God."

kids.question: When we judge others for doing wrong, what does Paul say we often do?
kids.option_a: The same things we judge others for
kids.explanation: Romans 2:1 — "Thou art inexcusable, O man, whosoever thou art that judgest: for wherein thou judgest another, thou condemnest thyself; for thou that judgest doest the same things."

kids.question: What does God look at — our outside or our heart?
kids.option_a: Our heart — He sees what is hidden inside
kids.explanation: Romans 2:29 — "But he is a Jew, which is one inwardly; and circumcision is that of the heart, in the spirit."

teens.question: What does the goodness of God lead people to?
teens.option_a: Repentance
teens.explanation: Romans 2:4 — "Or despisest thou the riches of his goodness and forbearance and longsuffering; not knowing that the goodness of God leadeth thee to repentance?"

teens.question: What are people treasuring up by their hard and impenitent hearts?
teens.option_a: Wrath against the day of wrath and revelation of the righteous judgment of God
teens.explanation: Romans 2:5 — "But after thy hardness and impenitent heart treasurest up unto thyself wrath against the day of wrath and revelation of the righteous judgment of God."

teens.question: What will God render to every man according to?
teens.option_a: His deeds (works)
teens.explanation: Romans 2:6 — "Who will render to every man according to his deeds."

teens.question: What does Paul say about Gentiles who do not have the law but do what it requires?
teens.option_a: They are a law unto themselves, showing the work of the law written in their hearts
teens.option_b: They are completely lost with no hope
teens.option_c: They are already saved without faith
teens.option_d: They do not need God at all
teens.explanation: Romans 2:14-15 — "For when the Gentiles, which have not the law, do by nature the things contained in the law, these... are a law unto themselves: Which shew the work of the law written in their hearts."

teens.question: What does Paul say about someone who teaches others but does not obey the teaching himself?
teens.option_a: Thou that teachest another, teachest thou not thyself? — he is a hypocrite
teens.option_b: He is still a great teacher
teens.option_c: His teaching is still valid and his behaviour does not matter
teens.explanation: Romans 2:21 — "Thou therefore which teachest another, teachest thou not thyself? thou that preachest a man should not steal, dost thou steal?"

adults.question: What principle does Paul establish about judging others?
adults.option_a: The one who judges another condemns himself, because he practises the same things he judges
adults.option_b: Judging is always wrong in every situation
adults.explanation: Romans 2:1 — "Therefore thou art inexcusable, O man, whosoever thou art that judgest: for wherein thou judgest another, thou condemnest thyself; for thou that judgest doest the same things."

adults.question: What does Paul say God will judge through Jesus Christ?
adults.option_a: The secrets of men
adults.option_d: Only the sins of the Jews
adults.explanation: Romans 2:16 — "In the day when God shall judge the secrets of men by Jesus Christ according to my gospel."

adults.question: How does Paul argue that possession of the law does not justify the Jews?
adults.option_a: Not the hearers of the law are just before God, but the doers of the law shall be justified
adults.option_b: Having the law automatically makes one righteous
adults.option_c: The law was given only as literature
adults.explanation: Romans 2:13 — "For not the hearers of the law are just before God, but the doers of the law shall be justified."

adults.question: What accusation does Paul level against Jewish teachers who boast in the law?
adults.option_a: Through breaking the law they dishonour God — causing the name of God to be blasphemed among the Gentiles
adults.option_b: They are the finest examples of obedience
adults.option_c: They always keep the law perfectly
adults.option_d: They should teach the Gentiles more
adults.explanation: Romans 2:23-24 — "Thou that makest thy boast of the law, through breaking the law dishonourest thou God? For the name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles through you, as it is written."

adults.question: What Old Testament passage does Paul reference about God's name being blasphemed among the Gentiles?
adults.option_a: Isaiah 52:5 and Ezekiel 36:22 — Israel's disobedience caused Gentiles to speak evil of God
adults.option_b: Psalm 23 — The Lord is my shepherd
adults.explanation: Romans 2:24 — "For the name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles through you, as it is written" (cf. Isaiah 52:5; Ezekiel 36:22).

adults.question: What does Paul say about circumcision for the lawbreaker?
adults.option_a: Circumcision is made uncircumcision — the outward sign is worthless without obedience
adults.option_b: Circumcision always counts regardless of behaviour
adults.explanation: Romans 2:25 — "For circumcision verily profiteth, if thou keep the law: but if thou be a breaker of the law, thy circumcision is made uncircumcision."

adults.question: What is Paul's redefinition of a true Jew?
adults.option_a: He is a Jew which is one inwardly; and circumcision is that of the heart, in the spirit, not in the letter
adults.explanation: Romans 2:28-29 — "But he is a Jew, which is one inwardly; and circumcision is that of the heart, in the spirit, and not in the letter; whose praise is not of men, but of God."

adults.question: What does Paul's argument in chapter 2 accomplish in the overall structure of Romans?
adults.option_a: It removes the moral superiority of the Jew, showing that both Jew and Gentile stand equally condemned before God — setting up the universal need for grace in chapter 3
adults.option_b: It praises the Jewish people for their faithfulness
adults.option_c: It argues that Gentiles are more sinful than Jews
adults.option_d: It provides rules for church governance
adults.explanation: Romans 2 demolishes Jewish presumption of immunity from judgment, completing Paul's indictment: Gentiles (ch.1) and Jews (ch.2) are both under sin (3:9).

kids.question: How many people have sinned according to Paul?
kids.option_a: All have sinned
kids.explanation: Romans 3:23 — "For all have sinned, and come short of the glory of God."

kids.question: Can anyone be made right with God by following rules perfectly?
kids.option_a: No, no one can be justified by the deeds of the law
kids.option_b: Yes, if you try hard enough
kids.option_c: Yes, if you are a priest
kids.option_d: Yes, if you are born Jewish
kids.explanation: Romans 3:20 — "Therefore by the deeds of the law there shall no flesh be justified in his sight."

kids.question: How does God make people righteous?
kids.option_a: Through faith in Jesus Christ
kids.option_d: Through being born in the right family
kids.explanation: Romans 3:22 — "Even the righteousness of God which is by faith of Jesus Christ unto all and upon all them that believe."

teens.question: What advantage does Paul say the Jews had?
teens.option_a: They were entrusted with the oracles (words) of God
teens.explanation: Romans 3:2 — "Much every way: chiefly, because that unto them were committed the oracles of God."

teens.question: What does Paul conclude about Jews and Gentiles together?
teens.option_a: They are all under sin — there is none righteous, no, not one
teens.option_b: Jews are righteous but Gentiles are not
teens.option_c: Gentiles are righteous but Jews are not
teens.option_d: Both groups are basically good people
teens.explanation: Romans 3:9-10 — "We have before proved both Jews and Gentiles, that they are all under sin; As it is written, There is none righteous, no, not one."

teens.question: What does "come short of the glory of God" mean?
teens.option_a: Everyone has failed to meet God's perfect standard of holiness
teens.option_b: Some people almost reach God's glory
teens.explanation: Romans 3:23 — "For all have sinned, and come short of the glory of God." The Greek word means to fall short of a target, like an arrow missing its mark.

teens.question: What does Paul say the law brings knowledge of?
teens.option_a: Sin — by the law is the knowledge of sin
teens.explanation: Romans 3:20 — "For by the law is the knowledge of sin."

teens.question: How are believers justified according to verse 24?
teens.option_a: Freely by God's grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus
teens.option_b: By paying a large sum of money
teens.option_d: By being baptised in the Jordan River
teens.explanation: Romans 3:24 — "Being justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus."

adults.question: What chain of Old Testament quotations does Paul use in verses 10-18 to prove universal sinfulness?
adults.option_a: Psalm 14:1-3, Psalm 5:9, Psalm 140:3, Psalm 10:7, Isaiah 59:7-8, and Psalm 36:1 — showing total depravity from character to conduct
adults.explanation: Romans 3:10-18 — Paul strings together six OT passages to demonstrate that sin affects every faculty: throat, tongue, lips, mouth (speech), feet (conduct), and eyes (attitude toward God).

adults.question: What does the phrase "the righteousness of God without the law" mean?
adults.option_a: A righteousness from God that is received by faith apart from law-keeping — witnessed by the Law and the Prophets
adults.option_c: A new set of easier commandments
adults.option_d: The abolition of all moral standards
adults.explanation: Romans 3:21 — "But now the righteousness of God without the law is manifested, being witnessed by the law and the prophets."

adults.question: What does the Greek word "hilasterion" (propitiation) in verse 25 mean?
adults.option_a: A sacrifice that satisfies God's wrath against sin — Christ is the mercy seat where divine justice and mercy meet
adults.option_d: A symbolic gesture with no real effect
adults.explanation: Romans 3:25 — "Whom God hath set forth to be a propitiation through faith in his blood." Hilasterion echoes the OT mercy seat (Exodus 25:17-22) where atonement was made.

adults.question: What does Paul say God demonstrated by setting forth Christ as a propitiation?
adults.option_a: His righteousness — to declare His justice in passing over former sins and in justifying the one who has faith in Jesus
adults.explanation: Romans 3:25-26 — "To declare his righteousness for the remission of sins that are past... that he might be just, and the justifier of him which believeth in Jesus."

adults.question: How does Paul answer the question "Where is boasting then?"
adults.option_a: It is excluded — not by a law of works, but by the law of faith
adults.option_b: Boasting is still permitted for Jews
adults.option_c: Boasting is acceptable if based on good deeds
adults.option_d: Boasting is only wrong for Gentiles
adults.explanation: Romans 3:27 — "Where is boasting then? It is excluded. By what law? of works? Nay: but by the law of faith."

adults.question: What does Paul conclude about how a man is justified?
adults.option_a: By faith without the deeds of the law
adults.option_b: By faith plus works of the law
adults.explanation: Romans 3:28 — "Therefore we conclude that a man is justified by faith without the deeds of the law."

adults.question: How does Paul argue that God is God of both Jews and Gentiles?
adults.option_a: Since there is one God, He justifies both circumcision by faith and uncircumcision through faith
adults.option_b: Jews and Gentiles have separate gods
adults.option_c: Only Jews have access to God
adults.explanation: Romans 3:29-30 — "Is he the God of the Jews only? is he not also of the Gentiles? Yes, of the Gentiles also: Seeing it is one God, which shall justify the circumcision by faith, and uncircumcision through faith."

adults.question: Does justification by faith make the law void?
adults.option_a: No — Paul says "God forbid: yea, we establish the law" — faith upholds the true purpose of the law
adults.option_b: Yes, the law is completely abolished
adults.option_d: The law applies only to Gentiles now
adults.explanation: Romans 3:31 — "Do we then make void the law through faith? God forbid: yea, we establish the law." Faith fulfills what the law aimed at but could not achieve.

kids.question: Did Abraham earn God's approval by being good, or by believing God?
kids.option_a: Abraham believed God and it was counted to him for righteousness
kids.option_b: He earned it by working hard
kids.option_c: He earned it by being perfect
kids.option_d: He earned it by being rich
kids.explanation: Romans 4:3 — "Abraham believed God, and it was counted unto him for righteousness."

kids.question: What did God promise Abraham even though he was very old?
kids.option_a: That he would be the father of many nations
kids.option_c: That he would be a king
kids.option_d: That he would never be sick
kids.explanation: Romans 4:17 — "I have made thee a father of many nations."

kids.question: Was Abraham's faith weak or strong?
kids.option_a: Strong — he was fully persuaded that God could do what He promised
kids.option_b: Weak — he did not believe God
kids.option_c: He had no faith at all
kids.explanation: Romans 4:20-21 — "He staggered not at the promise of God through unbelief; but was strong in faith, giving glory to God; And being fully persuaded that, what he had promised, he was able also to perform."

teens.question: What does Paul say about the person who works for wages versus the one who believes?
teens.option_a: To him that worketh not, but believeth on Him that justifieth the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness
teens.option_c: Both work and faith are equally required
teens.explanation: Romans 4:5 — "But to him that worketh not, but believeth on him that justifieth the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness."

teens.question: Which Old Testament figure does Paul cite alongside Abraham as an example of faith-righteousness?
teens.option_a: David — who described the blessedness of the man to whom God imputeth righteousness without works
teens.explanation: Romans 4:6 — "Even as David also describeth the blessedness of the man, unto whom God imputeth righteousness without works."

teens.question: Was Abraham justified before or after circumcision?
teens.option_a: Before — he received circumcision as a seal of the righteousness he had by faith while uncircumcised
teens.option_c: At the same time as circumcision
teens.option_d: Circumcision was unrelated to his faith
teens.explanation: Romans 4:10-11 — "How was it then reckoned? when he was in circumcision, or in uncircumcision? Not in circumcision, but in uncircumcision."

teens.question: What does Paul say the promise to Abraham came through?
teens.option_a: The righteousness of faith, not through the law
teens.explanation: Romans 4:13 — "For the promise, that he should be the heir of the world, was not to Abraham, or to his seed, through the law, but through the righteousness of faith."

teens.question: What did Abraham consider about his own body?
teens.option_a: He considered not his own body now dead (being about 100 years old) — yet he believed God's promise
teens.option_b: He believed because he was young and strong
teens.option_c: He doubted because of his age
teens.option_d: He asked for a different promise
teens.explanation: Romans 4:19 — "And being not weak in faith, he considered not his own body now dead, when he was about an hundred years old, neither yet the deadness of Sara's womb."

adults.question: What Genesis passage does Paul quote to establish justification by faith?
adults.option_a: Genesis 15:6 — "Abraham believed God, and it was counted unto him for righteousness"
adults.option_d: Genesis 12:1 — "Get thee out of thy country"
adults.explanation: Romans 4:3 — "For what saith the scripture? Abraham believed God, and it was counted unto him for righteousness" (Genesis 15:6).

adults.question: What Psalm does Paul quote in verses 7-8 about forgiveness?
adults.option_a: Psalm 32:1-2 — "Blessed are they whose iniquities are forgiven, and whose sins are covered. Blessed is the man to whom the Lord will not impute sin"
adults.option_b: Psalm 23 — The Lord is my shepherd
adults.option_c: Psalm 51 — Create in me a clean heart
adults.option_d: Psalm 119 — Thy word is a lamp
adults.explanation: Romans 4:7-8 — "Blessed are they whose iniquities are forgiven, and whose sins are covered. Blessed is the man to whom the Lord will not impute sin" (Psalm 32:1-2).

adults.question: Why is the timing of Abraham's justification (before circumcision) theologically important?
adults.option_a: It proves that righteousness comes by faith alone, not by any ritual — making Abraham the father of all who believe, whether circumcised or uncircumcised
adults.option_c: It shows Abraham was a Gentile
adults.option_d: It proves the law existed before Moses
adults.explanation: Romans 4:10-12 — Abraham was justified in Genesis 15, circumcised in Genesis 17 — approximately 14 years later — proving faith precedes and is independent of the sign.

adults.question: What does Paul mean by saying the law "worketh wrath"?
adults.option_a: The law reveals sin and brings condemnation — it cannot produce righteousness, only expose the need for it
adults.option_b: The law makes God angry at the law itself
adults.option_d: The law has no purpose at all
adults.explanation: Romans 4:15 — "Because the law worketh wrath: for where no law is, there is no transgression."

adults.question: How does Paul describe the God in whom Abraham believed?
adults.option_a: God who quickeneth (gives life to) the dead and calleth those things which be not as though they were
adults.option_b: A God who only blesses the worthy
adults.option_c: A God who requires perfect obedience before acting
adults.option_d: A God limited by natural laws
adults.explanation: Romans 4:17 — "God, who quickeneth the dead, and calleth those things which be not as though they were."

adults.question: What does "against hope believed in hope" mean regarding Abraham?
adults.option_a: When there was no human reason to hope, Abraham believed God's promise based on divine faithfulness alone
adults.explanation: Romans 4:18 — "Who against hope believed in hope, that he might become the father of many nations, according to that which was spoken, So shall thy seed be."

adults.question: What does Paul say about the imputation of righteousness being written for Abraham's sake alone?
adults.option_a: It was not written for his sake alone, but for us also — to whom righteousness shall be imputed if we believe on Him that raised Jesus from the dead
adults.option_c: It was a unique case never to be repeated
adults.option_d: It was relevant only to the Jewish nation
adults.explanation: Romans 4:23-24 — "Now it was not written for his sake alone, that it was imputed to him; But for us also, to whom it shall be imputed, if we believe on him that raised up Jesus our Lord from the dead."

adults.question: How does Paul connect Jesus' death and resurrection to justification in verse 25?
adults.option_a: Jesus was delivered for our offences and raised again for our justification — His death paid for sin and His resurrection secured our righteous standing
adults.option_b: Only His death matters for salvation
adults.option_c: Only His resurrection matters for salvation
adults.option_d: Neither His death nor resurrection is related to justification
adults.explanation: Romans 4:25 — "Who was delivered for our offences, and was raised again for our justification." Christ's death addresses the penalty of sin; His resurrection confirms the Father's acceptance of that sacrifice.

kids.question: What do we have with God through Jesus Christ?
kids.option_a: Peace with God
kids.explanation: Romans 5:1 — "Therefore being justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ."

kids.question: Who brought sin into the world?
kids.option_a: Adam — one man's disobedience brought sin
kids.explanation: Romans 5:12 — "Wherefore, as by one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin."

kids.question: What did God show us by sending Jesus to die for us?
kids.option_a: His love for us — Christ died for us while we were still sinners
kids.option_b: That He was angry with us
kids.option_c: That we had to save ourselves
kids.explanation: Romans 5:8 — "But God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us."

teens.question: What does tribulation produce according to Paul's chain?
teens.option_a: Patience — and patience produces experience, and experience produces hope
teens.explanation: Romans 5:3-4 — "Tribulation worketh patience; And patience, experience; and experience, hope."

teens.question: What has been shed abroad in our hearts?
teens.option_a: The love of God by the Holy Ghost which is given unto us
teens.explanation: Romans 5:5 — "And hope maketh not ashamed; because the love of God is shed abroad in our hearts by the Holy Ghost which is given unto us."

teens.question: What is remarkable about the timing of Christ's death?
teens.option_a: Christ died for us while we were yet sinners — not after we became good
teens.option_b: Christ died only for righteous people
teens.option_c: Christ died after people earned salvation
teens.explanation: Romans 5:8 — "But God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us."

teens.question: How did death spread to all men?
teens.option_a: By one man (Adam) sin entered the world, and death by sin — so death passed upon all men, for that all have sinned
teens.option_b: Death was always part of God's plan
teens.option_d: Death came through natural causes alone
teens.explanation: Romans 5:12 — "Wherefore, as by one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin; and so death passed upon all men, for that all have sinned."

teens.question: How does Paul contrast Adam and Christ?
teens.option_a: Adam's one offence brought death to all, but Christ's one act of righteousness brings the free gift of life to all who believe
teens.option_b: Both Adam and Christ brought equal harm
teens.option_c: Adam was more important than Christ
teens.option_d: Christ merely reversed Adam's physical death
teens.explanation: Romans 5:18 — "Therefore as by the offence of one judgment came upon all men to condemnation; even so by the righteousness of one the free gift came upon all men unto justification of life."

adults.question: What two benefits of justification does Paul name in verses 1-2?
adults.option_a: Peace with God and access by faith into the grace wherein we stand — plus rejoicing in hope of the glory of God
adults.option_c: Freedom from all suffering and immediate glorification
adults.explanation: Romans 5:1-2 — "Being justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ: By whom also we have access by faith into this grace wherein we stand, and rejoice in hope of the glory of God."

adults.question: What does Paul say about God's demonstration of love versus human love?
adults.option_a: Scarcely for a righteous man will one die, but God commendeth His love in that Christ died for us while we were yet sinners
adults.option_b: Human love is equal to God's love
adults.option_c: God's love is conditional on our merit
adults.option_d: Humans love more sacrificially than God
adults.explanation: Romans 5:7-8 — "For scarcely for a righteous man will one die: yet peradventure for a good man some would even dare to die. But God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us."

adults.question: What is the logical argument of "much more" in verses 9-10?
adults.option_a: If we were justified by Christ's blood while enemies, much more shall we be saved from wrath and by His life now that we are reconciled
adults.option_b: Salvation is uncertain and may be lost
adults.option_d: God's anger may return at any time
adults.explanation: Romans 5:9-10 — "Much more then, being now justified by his blood, we shall be saved from wrath through him. For if, when we were enemies, we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son, much more, being reconciled, we shall be saved by his life."

adults.question: What does the Adam-Christ typology in verses 12-21 teach about federal headship?
adults.option_a: Adam acted as the representative head of all humanity in sin, just as Christ acts as the representative head of all who believe — one man's act determines the standing of many
adults.option_b: Each person's sin is independent of Adam
adults.option_c: Christ and Adam are unrelated figures
adults.option_d: Federal headship is a modern invention
adults.explanation: Romans 5:19 — "For as by one man's disobedience many were made sinners, so by the obedience of one shall many be made righteous." Adam and Christ are federal (covenant) heads.

adults.question: What does Paul say about the law entering in verse 20?
adults.option_a: The law entered that the offence might abound — but where sin abounded, grace did much more abound
adults.explanation: Romans 5:20 — "Moreover the law entered, that the offence might abound. But where sin abounded, grace did much more abound."

adults.question: What does the term "free gift" (charisma) emphasize about salvation?
adults.option_a: Salvation is entirely unmerited — a gracious gift from God, not earned or deserved
adults.option_d: The gift is available only to some
adults.explanation: Romans 5:15-16 — "But not as the offence, so also is the free gift... the free gift is of many offences unto justification." The repeated term charisma stresses grace.

adults.question: How does Paul say believers shall "reign in life"?
adults.option_a: They which receive abundance of grace and of the gift of righteousness shall reign in life by one, Jesus Christ
adults.explanation: Romans 5:17 — "For if by one man's offence death reigned by one; much more they which receive abundance of grace and of the gift of righteousness shall reign in life by one, Jesus Christ."

adults.question: What is the ultimate purpose of grace according to verse 21?
adults.option_a: That as sin hath reigned unto death, even so might grace reign through righteousness unto eternal life by Jesus Christ our Lord
adults.option_d: Grace is limited to this earthly life
adults.explanation: Romans 5:21 — "That as sin hath reigned unto death, even so might grace reign through righteousness unto eternal life by Jesus Christ our Lord."

kids.question: Should we keep sinning so that God gives us more grace?
kids.option_a: No! God forbid — we should not continue in sin
kids.option_b: Yes, more sin means more grace
kids.option_c: It does not matter either way
kids.option_d: Only a little sinning is fine
kids.explanation: Romans 6:1-2 — "Shall we continue in sin, that grace may abound? God forbid. How shall we, that are dead to sin, live any longer therein?"

kids.question: What does baptism picture according to Paul?
kids.option_a: Being buried with Jesus and rising to walk in a new life
kids.explanation: Romans 6:4 — "Therefore we are buried with him by baptism into death: that like as Christ was raised up from the dead by the glory of the Father, even so we also should walk in newness of life."

kids.question: What is the wages (payment) of sin?
kids.option_a: Death
kids.explanation: Romans 6:23 — "For the wages of sin is death; but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord."

teens.question: What does Paul say our old man was crucified with Christ for?
teens.option_a: That the body of sin might be destroyed, that henceforth we should not serve sin
teens.option_b: So we could keep sinning freely
teens.option_c: So the law would be abolished
teens.option_d: So we could earn our salvation
teens.explanation: Romans 6:6 — "Knowing this, that our old man is crucified with him, that the body of sin might be destroyed, that henceforth we should not serve sin."

teens.question: What should believers reckon (consider) themselves to be?
teens.option_a: Dead indeed unto sin, but alive unto God through Jesus Christ our Lord
teens.option_b: Dead to God and alive to sin
teens.option_d: Alive to both sin and God equally
teens.explanation: Romans 6:11 — "Likewise reckon ye also yourselves to be dead indeed unto sin, but alive unto God through Jesus Christ our Lord."

teens.question: What should believers yield their members (bodies) as?
teens.option_a: Instruments of righteousness unto God
teens.explanation: Romans 6:13 — "Neither yield ye your members as instruments of unrighteousness unto sin: but yield yourselves unto God... and your members as instruments of righteousness unto God."

teens.question: What are believers now servants (slaves) of?
teens.option_a: Righteousness — having been set free from sin
teens.option_b: Sin — having been set free from righteousness
teens.explanation: Romans 6:18 — "Being then made free from sin, ye became the servants of righteousness."

teens.question: What is the gift of God contrasted with the wages of sin?
teens.option_a: Eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord
teens.explanation: Romans 6:23 — "For the wages of sin is death; but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord."

adults.question: How does Paul refute the idea that grace encourages sin (antinomianism)?
adults.option_a: Believers have died to sin through union with Christ in His death — they cannot continue living in what they died to
adults.option_c: He admits grace may lead to more sin
adults.option_d: He restricts grace to avoid the problem
adults.explanation: Romans 6:2-3 — "God forbid. How shall we, that are dead to sin, live any longer therein? Know ye not, that so many of us as were baptized into Jesus Christ were baptized into his death?"

adults.question: What does baptism symbolise in Paul's theology?
adults.option_a: Union with Christ in His death, burial, and resurrection — the believer's identification with Christ's redemptive work
adults.option_d: A public declaration with no deeper meaning
adults.explanation: Romans 6:3-4 — "Buried with him by baptism into death: that like as Christ was raised up from the dead by the glory of the Father, even so we also should walk in newness of life."

adults.question: What does "our old man is crucified with him" mean?
adults.option_a: The believer's former unregenerate self was judicially put to death with Christ so that the power of sin over the body is broken
adults.option_b: The physical body dies at conversion
adults.option_c: Believers never struggle with sin again
adults.option_d: The old nature ceases to exist entirely
adults.explanation: Romans 6:6 — "Knowing this, that our old man is crucified with him, that the body of sin might be destroyed, that henceforth we should not serve sin."

adults.question: What logical argument does Paul make from Christ's resurrection in verse 9?
adults.option_a: Christ being raised from the dead dieth no more; death hath no more dominion over Him — and since believers are united with Him, sin has no dominion over them either
adults.option_d: Death still has power over Christ
adults.explanation: Romans 6:9 — "Knowing that Christ being raised from the dead dieth no more; death hath no more dominion over him."

adults.question: What does "sin shall not have dominion over you" depend on?
adults.option_a: Being under grace, not under law — grace provides both the motive and the power to overcome sin
adults.explanation: Romans 6:14 — "For sin shall not have dominion over you: for ye are not under the law, but under grace."

adults.question: How does Paul use the slave-master metaphor to explain sanctification?
adults.option_a: Just as a slave obeys one master, believers have changed masters — from sin (leading to death) to obedience (leading to righteousness)
adults.option_c: Believers have no master at all
adults.option_d: Slavery is endorsed as a social institution
adults.explanation: Romans 6:16-18 — "Know ye not, that to whom ye yield yourselves servants to obey, his servants ye are... Being then made free from sin, ye became the servants of righteousness."

adults.question: What is the "fruit" of the old life versus the new life?
adults.option_a: The old life produced things they are now ashamed of, ending in death; the new life produces fruit unto holiness, ending in everlasting life
adults.option_b: Both lives produce the same fruit
adults.option_c: The old life was productive and good
adults.option_d: The new life has no visible results
adults.explanation: Romans 6:21-22 — "What fruit had ye then in those things whereof ye are now ashamed? for the end of those things is death. But now being made free from sin... ye have your fruit unto holiness, and the end everlasting life."

adults.question: Why does Paul use the word "wages" for sin but "gift" for eternal life?
adults.option_a: Sin pays what is earned and deserved (death); but eternal life is unearned — a free gift (charisma) of God through Jesus Christ
adults.explanation: Romans 6:23 — "For the wages of sin is death; but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord." The deliberate contrast between earned wages and free gift encapsulates the gospel.

kids.question: Did Paul say the law is bad?
kids.option_a: No, the law is holy, just, and good
kids.option_d: The law is only for children
kids.explanation: Romans 7:12 — "Wherefore the law is holy, and the commandment holy, and just, and good."

kids.question: What struggle did Paul describe about doing right?
kids.option_a: The good that he wanted to do, he did not do; and the bad he did not want to do, he kept doing
kids.option_b: He always did the right thing perfectly
kids.explanation: Romans 7:19 — "For the good that I would I do not: but the evil which I would not, that I do."

kids.question: Who did Paul say could rescue him from this struggle?
kids.option_a: Jesus Christ our Lord
kids.explanation: Romans 7:25 — "I thank God through Jesus Christ our Lord."

teens.question: What illustration does Paul use from marriage law to explain freedom from the law?
teens.option_a: A woman is bound to her husband while he lives, but free when he dies — likewise believers have died to the law through Christ's body
teens.option_b: Marriage has nothing to do with the law
teens.option_c: Divorce frees people from all obligations
teens.explanation: Romans 7:2-4 — "For the woman which hath an husband is bound by the law to her husband so long as he liveth; but if the husband be dead, she is loosed from the law of her husband."

teens.question: What did the law cause sin to do in Paul?
teens.option_a: It produced all manner of evil desire — for without the law, sin was dead
teens.option_c: It had no effect on sin
teens.explanation: Romans 7:8 — "But sin, taking occasion by the commandment, wrought in me all manner of concupiscence. For without the law sin was dead."

teens.question: What commandment did Paul cite as an example?
teens.option_a: Thou shalt not covet
teens.explanation: Romans 7:7 — "I had not known sin, but by the law: for I had not known lust, except the law had said, Thou shalt not covet."

teens.question: What does Paul say about his inner man versus his flesh?
teens.option_a: He delights in the law of God after the inward man, but sees another law in his members warring against the law of his mind
teens.option_b: His inner man and flesh are in perfect harmony
teens.option_d: There is no conflict in the Christian life
teens.explanation: Romans 7:22-23 — "For I delight in the law of God after the inward man: But I see another law in my members, warring against the law of my mind."

teens.question: What cry does Paul make at the climax of his struggle?
teens.option_a: O wretched man that I am! who shall deliver me from the body of this death?
teens.option_b: I am perfect and need no help
teens.option_c: I have conquered all sin by myself
teens.option_d: There is no real problem to solve
teens.explanation: Romans 7:24 — "O wretched man that I am! who shall deliver me from the body of this death?"

adults.question: How does Paul's marriage analogy in verses 1-4 establish freedom from the law?
adults.option_a: Just as death dissolves a marriage bond, believers have died to the law through the body of Christ, freeing them to be joined to the risen Christ and bear fruit unto God
adults.option_b: Believers divorce the law through a legal process
adults.explanation: Romans 7:4 — "Wherefore, my brethren, ye also are become dead to the law by the body of Christ; that ye should be married to another, even to him who is raised from the dead, that we should bring forth fruit unto God."

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "the motions of sins, which were by the law, did work in our members"?
adults.option_a: The law, rather than restraining sin, actually aroused sinful passions — the prohibition provoked the very desire it forbade
adults.option_b: The law successfully prevented all sin
adults.option_c: Sin existed independently of the law
adults.option_d: The law created sin out of nothing
adults.explanation: Romans 7:5 — "For when we were in the flesh, the motions of sins, which were by the law, did work in our members to bring forth fruit unto death."

adults.question: Is the law sin? How does Paul answer this?
adults.option_a: God forbid — the law is not sin, but it reveals sin; Paul would not have known coveting except through the commandment
adults.option_b: Yes, the law is inherently sinful
adults.option_c: The law is irrelevant to sin
adults.option_d: The question should never be asked
adults.explanation: Romans 7:7 — "What shall we say then? Is the law sin? God forbid. Nay, I had not known sin, but by the law."

adults.question: How does Paul describe sin's use of the commandment?
adults.option_a: Sin, taking occasion by the commandment, deceived him and by it slew him — sin is personified as a deceiver that hijacks the good law
adults.option_b: Sin and the commandment work together
adults.explanation: Romans 7:11 — "For sin, taking occasion by the commandment, deceived me, and by it slew me."

adults.question: What theological truth does Paul establish about the nature of the law in verse 14?
adults.option_a: The law is spiritual, but I am carnal, sold under sin — the problem is not the law but fallen human nature
adults.option_b: The law is carnal and man is spiritual
adults.option_c: Both the law and man are equally flawed
adults.option_d: The law has no spiritual dimension
adults.explanation: Romans 7:14 — "For we know that the law is spiritual: but I am carnal, sold under sin."

adults.question: What is the "law of sin" that Paul identifies in verse 23?
adults.option_a: A principle of indwelling sin operating in his members, warring against the law of his mind and bringing him into captivity
adults.option_b: A literal legal code requiring sinning
adults.option_d: An external force imposed by Satan alone
adults.explanation: Romans 7:23 — "But I see another law in my members, warring against the law of my mind, and bringing me into captivity to the law of sin which is in my members."

adults.question: Is Paul describing his pre-conversion or post-conversion experience in verses 14-25? What are the arguments?
adults.option_a: The use of present tense, the delight in God's law, and the struggle with indwelling sin strongly suggest Paul describes the ongoing struggle of a regenerate believer — though some argue it is pre-conversion or a rhetorical device
adults.option_c: It describes someone who was never a believer
adults.explanation: Romans 7:22,25 — "I delight in the law of God after the inward man... So then with the mind I myself serve the law of God." The delight in God's law and serving it with the mind point to regenerate experience.

adults.question: How does verse 25 serve as a transition to chapter 8?
adults.option_a: Paul thanks God through Jesus Christ — acknowledging that while the struggle with indwelling sin remains, deliverance comes through Christ, leading to the triumph declared in chapter 8
adults.option_c: It concludes the entire argument of Romans
adults.option_d: It introduces a new unrelated topic
adults.explanation: Romans 7:25 — "I thank God through Jesus Christ our Lord." This thanksgiving points forward to chapter 8's declaration of no condemnation and life in the Spirit.

kids.question: Is there any condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus?
kids.option_a: No, there is no condemnation
kids.explanation: Romans 8:1 — "There is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus."

kids.question: What does the Spirit of God help us call God?
kids.option_a: Abba, Father — meaning dear Father
kids.explanation: Romans 8:15 — "Ye have received the Spirit of adoption, whereby we cry, Abba, Father."

kids.question: Can anything separate us from God's love?
kids.option_a: No, nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus
kids.option_d: Yes, if we are not good enough
kids.explanation: Romans 8:38-39 — "For I am persuaded, that neither death, nor life... shall be able to separate us from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord."

teens.question: What could the law not do, and what did God do instead?
teens.option_a: What the law could not do because of the weakness of the flesh, God did by sending His own Son to condemn sin in the flesh
teens.option_d: The law was replaced by a new set of rules
teens.explanation: Romans 8:3 — "For what the law could not do, in that it was weak through the flesh, God sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh, and for sin, condemned sin in the flesh."

teens.question: What does it mean to be carnally minded versus spiritually minded?
teens.option_a: To be carnally minded is death, but to be spiritually minded is life and peace
teens.option_c: Carnal mindedness is better for daily life
teens.option_d: Spiritual mindedness only matters after death
teens.explanation: Romans 8:6 — "For to be carnally minded is death; but to be spiritually minded is life and peace."

teens.question: What does Paul say all things work together for?
teens.option_a: Good to them that love God, to them who are the called according to His purpose
teens.option_d: Only spiritual things, not practical ones
teens.explanation: Romans 8:28 — "And we know that all things work together for good to them that love God, to them who are the called according to his purpose."

teens.question: If God be for us, who can be against us?
teens.option_a: No one can successfully stand against us — He that spared not His own Son will freely give us all things
teens.option_b: Many people can be effectively against us
teens.option_c: God is not always for us
teens.option_d: Only the strong can be against us
teens.explanation: Romans 8:31-32 — "If God be for us, who can be against us? He that spared not his own Son, but delivered him up for us all, how shall he not with him also freely give us all things?"

teens.question: What is the list of things that cannot separate us from God's love?
teens.option_a: Death, life, angels, principalities, powers, things present, things to come, height, depth, nor any other creature
teens.explanation: Romans 8:38-39 — "For I am persuaded, that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor principalities, nor powers, nor things present, nor things to come, Nor height, nor depth, nor any other creature, shall be able to separate us from the love of God."

adults.question: What does "no condemnation" in verse 1 mean in context of Paul's argument?
adults.option_a: After establishing universal guilt (ch.1-3), justification by faith (ch.3-5), and the believer's struggle with sin (ch.7), Paul declares that those in Christ face no judicial condemnation — their legal standing before God is fully secure
adults.option_b: Believers will never experience any difficulty
adults.option_c: Sin no longer exists for Christians
adults.explanation: Romans 8:1 — "There is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus." This is the triumphant conclusion: justified believers stand free from God's condemning verdict.

adults.question: What is the "law of the Spirit of life" that has freed believers?
adults.option_a: The governing principle of the Holy Spirit's life-giving power, which overcomes the principle of sin and death operating in fallen human nature
adults.option_b: A new set of written commandments
adults.explanation: Romans 8:2 — "For the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus hath made me free from the law of sin and death."

adults.question: What does Paul teach about the Holy Spirit's role in assurance of salvation?
adults.option_a: The Spirit itself beareth witness with our spirit that we are the children of God — providing internal testimony of our adoption
adults.option_b: The Spirit has no role in assurance
adults.option_c: Assurance comes only from good works
adults.explanation: Romans 8:16 — "The Spirit itself beareth witness with our spirit, that we are the children of God."

adults.question: What does Paul say about present sufferings compared to future glory?
adults.option_a: The sufferings of this present time are not worthy to be compared with the glory which shall be revealed in us
adults.option_c: There will be no future glory
adults.explanation: Romans 8:18 — "For I reckon that the sufferings of this present time are not worthy to be compared with the glory which shall be revealed in us."

adults.question: What does Paul say creation is waiting for?
adults.option_a: The manifestation of the sons of God — when believers are glorified and creation itself is delivered from the bondage of corruption
adults.option_d: The end of all animal life
adults.explanation: Romans 8:19,21 — "For the earnest expectation of the creature waiteth for the manifestation of the sons of God... the creature itself also shall be delivered from the bondage of corruption into the glorious liberty of the children of God."

adults.question: What is the "golden chain of salvation" in verses 29-30?
adults.option_a: Foreknew, predestinated, called, justified, glorified — an unbreakable chain from God's eternal purpose to the believer's final glorification
adults.explanation: Romans 8:29-30 — "For whom he did foreknow, he also did predestinate to be conformed to the image of his Son... Moreover whom he did predestinate, them he also called: and whom he called, them he also justified: and whom he justified, them he also glorified."

adults.question: How does Paul use five rhetorical questions in verses 31-35 to establish the security of the believer?
adults.option_a: Each question expects a resounding answer: no one can condemn (Christ intercedes), no one can separate (God's love is supreme), nothing can defeat the purpose of God for His people
adults.option_b: The questions express doubt about salvation
adults.option_c: The questions are addressed to unbelievers
adults.option_d: The questions have no clear answers
adults.explanation: Romans 8:31-35 — "If God be for us, who can be against us?... Who shall lay any thing to the charge of God's elect?... Who shall separate us from the love of Christ?" Each builds an unassailable case for eternal security.

adults.question: How does Romans 8:28 relate to God's sovereignty and human suffering?
adults.option_a: God sovereignly orchestrates all circumstances — including suffering — to accomplish His good purpose for those who love Him and are called according to His purpose, conforming them to the image of His Son
adults.option_c: Suffering is always punishment for sin
adults.option_d: God is unaware of human suffering
adults.explanation: Romans 8:28-29 — "All things work together for good to them that love God... to be conformed to the image of his Son." God's providence encompasses all events, directing them toward the ultimate good of Christlikeness.

kids.question: How much did Paul care about his fellow Israelites?
kids.option_a: So much that he wished he could be cut off from Christ for their sake
kids.option_b: He did not care about them
kids.option_d: He wanted them to be punished
kids.explanation: Romans 9:3 — "For I could wish that myself were accursed from Christ for my brethren, my kinsmen according to the flesh."

kids.question: Does God have the right to do whatever He wants, like a potter with clay?
kids.option_a: Yes, the potter has power over the clay to make what he chooses
kids.option_b: No, the clay tells the potter what to do
kids.option_c: The potter and clay are equal
kids.option_d: The clay has more power than the potter
kids.explanation: Romans 9:21 — "Hath not the potter power over the clay, of the same lump to make one vessel unto honour, and another unto dishonour?"

kids.question: What did God say about Jacob and Esau before they were born?
kids.option_a: The elder shall serve the younger
kids.option_b: The younger shall serve the elder
kids.explanation: Romans 9:12 — "It was said unto her, The elder shall serve the younger."

teens.question: What special privileges did Israel receive from God?
teens.option_a: The adoption, the glory, the covenants, the giving of the law, the service of God, and the promises
teens.option_b: Only the law and nothing else
teens.explanation: Romans 9:4 — "Who are Israelites; to whom pertaineth the adoption, and the glory, and the covenants, and the giving of the law, and the service of God, and the promises."

teens.question: What principle does God's choice of Jacob over Esau illustrate?
teens.option_a: God's sovereign election — His purpose stands not of works but of Him that calleth
teens.option_b: That Jacob was morally better than Esau
teens.option_c: That older children are less important
teens.option_d: That twins should always be separated
teens.explanation: Romans 9:11 — "That the purpose of God according to election might stand, not of works, but of him that calleth."

teens.question: What does God say to Moses about mercy?
teens.option_a: I will have mercy on whom I will have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I will have compassion
teens.option_b: I will have mercy only on Israel
teens.option_c: Mercy is given based on good works
teens.explanation: Romans 9:15 — "For he saith to Moses, I will have mercy on whom I will have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I will have compassion."

teens.question: What does Paul say about God raising up Pharaoh?
teens.option_a: God raised him up to show His power in him and that His name might be declared throughout all the earth
teens.option_b: Pharaoh rose to power by accident
teens.option_c: God had no involvement with Pharaoh
teens.explanation: Romans 9:17 — "For the scripture saith unto Pharaoh, Even for this same purpose have I raised thee up, that I might shew my power in thee, and that my name might be declared throughout all the earth."

teens.question: What stumbling stone did Israel stumble over?
teens.option_a: Christ — they sought righteousness by works of the law instead of by faith
teens.option_b: A literal stone in the road
teens.explanation: Romans 9:32-33 — "They stumbled at that stumblingstone; As it is written, Behold, I lay in Sion a stumblingstone and rock of offence."

adults.question: How does Paul's statement about wishing himself accursed (anathema) demonstrate his pastoral heart?
adults.option_a: Paul would exchange his own salvation for Israel's — echoing Moses' similar plea (Exodus 32:32), showing the depth of apostolic love for his people
adults.option_b: Paul was being hyperbolic and insincere
adults.option_d: Paul wanted to return to Judaism
adults.explanation: Romans 9:3 — "For I could wish that myself were accursed from Christ for my brethren, my kinsmen according to the flesh." This echoes Moses' plea in Exodus 32:32.

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "not all Israel are of Israel" in verse 6?
adults.option_a: Physical descent from Abraham does not guarantee membership in the true spiritual Israel — God's promise applies to the children of promise, not merely of the flesh
adults.option_c: Israel as a nation has been replaced
adults.option_d: Only half of Israel is truly Israel
adults.explanation: Romans 9:6-8 — "They are not all Israel, which are of Israel: Neither, because they are the seed of Abraham, are they all children: but, In Isaac shall thy seed be called."

adults.question: How does Paul respond to the objection "Is there unrighteousness with God?"
adults.option_a: God forbid — Paul appeals to God's sovereign right to show mercy or harden as He wills, quoting Exodus 33:19 and Exodus 9:16
adults.option_b: He admits there may be some unfairness
adults.option_d: He says the question is irrelevant
adults.explanation: Romans 9:14-17 — "Is there unrighteousness with God? God forbid. For he saith to Moses, I will have mercy on whom I will have mercy."

adults.question: What does the potter-clay analogy teach about God's sovereignty?
adults.option_a: God as Creator has absolute right over His creation — to make vessels of honour or dishonour from the same lump, displaying both His wrath and His mercy
adults.option_d: God creates some people solely for destruction
adults.explanation: Romans 9:20-23 — "Shall the thing formed say to him that formed it, Why hast thou made me thus? Hath not the potter power over the clay?" The passage emphasises God's right, not His arbitrariness.

adults.question: What Hosea prophecy does Paul apply to the inclusion of Gentiles?
adults.option_a: Hosea 2:23 and 1:10 — "I will call them my people, which were not my people; and her beloved, which was not beloved" — showing God's plan always included Gentiles
adults.option_c: A prophecy from Ezekiel about dry bones
adults.explanation: Romans 9:25-26 — "I will call them my people, which were not my people; and her beloved, which was not beloved. And it shall come to pass, that in the place where it was said unto them, Ye are not my people; there shall they be called the children of the living God."

adults.question: What Isaiah quotation does Paul use to show that only a remnant of Israel would be saved?
adults.option_a: Isaiah 10:22-23 — "Though the number of the children of Israel be as the sand of the sea, a remnant shall be saved"
adults.option_c: Isaiah 40:31 — they that wait upon the Lord
adults.explanation: Romans 9:27 — "Esaias also crieth concerning Israel, Though the number of the children of Israel be as the sand of the sea, a remnant shall be saved."

adults.question: Why did Israel fail to attain righteousness according to verse 32?
adults.option_a: Because they sought it not by faith, but as it were by the works of the law — they stumbled at the stumbling stone
adults.option_b: Because they did not have the law
adults.option_c: Because they were not descended from Abraham
adults.option_d: Because they lived outside the promised land
adults.explanation: Romans 9:32 — "Wherefore? Because they sought it not by faith, but as it were by the works of the law. For they stumbled at that stumblingstone."

adults.question: How does Romans 9 balance divine sovereignty and human responsibility?
adults.option_a: Paul presents God's sovereign election as the foundation of salvation while in chapters 9-10 he also presents Israel's unbelief as culpable — holding both truths in tension without resolving the mystery
adults.option_c: It teaches only human free will
adults.explanation: Romans 9-10 — Chapter 9 emphasises God's sovereign mercy (v.16), while chapter 10 emphasises Israel's wilful unbelief (10:21). Paul holds both truths without eliminating either.

kids.question: What should you confess with your mouth to be saved?
kids.option_a: That Jesus is Lord
kids.option_b: That you are a good person
kids.explanation: Romans 10:9 — "That if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised him from the dead, thou shalt be saved."

kids.question: Does God treat Jews and non-Jews differently when they call on Him?
kids.option_a: No, the same Lord is rich unto all that call upon Him
kids.option_b: Yes, He only helps Jewish people
kids.option_c: Yes, He only helps non-Jewish people
kids.explanation: Romans 10:12 — "For there is no difference between the Jew and the Greek: for the same Lord over all is rich unto all that call upon him."

kids.question: How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news?
kids.option_a: Very beautiful — How beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel of peace
kids.option_c: Only if they wear nice shoes
kids.option_d: Only if they are fast runners
kids.explanation: Romans 10:15 — "How beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel of peace, and bring glad tidings of good things!"

teens.question: What is the difference between Israel's zeal and true knowledge?
teens.option_a: They had a zeal of God, but not according to knowledge — seeking their own righteousness instead of submitting to God's
teens.option_b: Their zeal and knowledge were perfectly balanced
teens.option_c: They had no zeal at all
teens.option_d: Their knowledge was greater than their zeal
teens.explanation: Romans 10:2-3 — "For I bear them record that they have a zeal of God, but not according to knowledge. For they being ignorant of God's righteousness, and going about to establish their own righteousness, have not submitted themselves unto the righteousness of God."

teens.question: Who is the end (goal/fulfilment) of the law for righteousness?
teens.option_a: Christ — for righteousness to every one that believeth
teens.explanation: Romans 10:4 — "For Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth."

teens.question: What two things does Romans 10:9 say are needed for salvation?
teens.option_a: Confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus and believe in thine heart that God hath raised Him from the dead
teens.option_b: Keep all the commandments and do good works
teens.option_c: Be baptised and join a church
teens.option_d: Give all your money and fast every day
teens.explanation: Romans 10:9 — "That if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised him from the dead, thou shalt be saved."

teens.question: What logical chain does Paul build about hearing the gospel?
teens.option_a: They cannot call on one they have not believed; they cannot believe without hearing; they cannot hear without a preacher; and a preacher must be sent
teens.explanation: Romans 10:14-15 — "How then shall they call on him in whom they have not believed? and how shall they believe in him of whom they have not heard? and how shall they hear without a preacher? And how shall they preach, except they be sent?"

teens.question: How does faith come according to verse 17?
teens.option_a: Faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God
teens.option_d: Faith comes naturally without any help
teens.explanation: Romans 10:17 — "So then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God."

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "Christ is the end of the law" (telos)?
adults.option_a: Christ is both the goal (fulfilment) and the termination of the law as a means of achieving righteousness — He accomplished what the law pointed to but could not achieve
adults.option_c: The law continues unchanged for believers
adults.option_d: Christ merely added to the law
adults.explanation: Romans 10:4 — "For Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth." The Greek telos means both goal/aim and conclusion.

adults.question: What Deuteronomy passage does Paul reinterpret in verses 6-8?
adults.option_a: Deuteronomy 30:12-14 — Paul applies Moses' words about the accessibility of the law to the accessibility of Christ and the gospel message
adults.explanation: Romans 10:6-8 — Paul draws on Deuteronomy 30:12-14, reinterpreting the "word" that is near (in thy mouth and heart) as the word of faith that he preaches.

adults.question: What is the relationship between heart belief and mouth confession in verse 9?
adults.option_a: Heart belief in the resurrection secures righteousness before God, while mouth confession demonstrates and expresses that inward faith publicly — both are aspects of genuine saving faith
adults.option_c: Belief alone saves without any expression
adults.explanation: Romans 10:9-10 — "With the heart man believeth unto righteousness; and with the mouth confession is made unto salvation." Internal faith and external confession are two sides of the same reality.

adults.question: What Joel prophecy does Paul cite to prove universal access to salvation?
adults.option_a: Joel 2:32 — "Whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" — extending the promise beyond ethnic boundaries
adults.option_d: Joel 2:28 — the outpouring of the Spirit
adults.explanation: Romans 10:13 — "For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" (Joel 2:32).

adults.question: What Isaiah passage does Paul quote about beautiful feet?
adults.option_a: Isaiah 52:7 — "How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him that bringeth good tidings, that publisheth peace"
adults.explanation: Romans 10:15 — "How beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel of peace" (Isaiah 52:7).

adults.question: How does Paul use Isaiah 53:1 to explain Israel's unbelief?
adults.option_a: "Lord, who hath believed our report?" — showing that unbelief in the gospel was prophesied and is not a failure of the message but of the hearers
adults.option_b: Isaiah 53:1 is about a different subject
adults.explanation: Romans 10:16 — "But they have not all obeyed the gospel. For Esaias saith, Lord, who hath believed our report?"

adults.question: How does Paul prove that Israel did hear the gospel?
adults.option_a: He quotes Psalm 19:4 — "Their sound went into all the earth, and their words unto the ends of the world" — Israel cannot claim ignorance
adults.option_b: Israel never had the opportunity to hear
adults.option_c: The gospel was preached only in Jerusalem
adults.explanation: Romans 10:18 — "But I say, Have they not heard? Yes verily, their sound went into all the earth, and their words unto the ends of the world."

adults.question: What does Paul quote from Isaiah 65:2 about God's posture toward Israel?
adults.option_a: "All day long I have stretched forth my hands unto a disobedient and gainsaying people" — God persistently offered grace, but Israel stubbornly refused
adults.option_c: God never reached out to Israel
adults.explanation: Romans 10:21 — "But to Israel he saith, All day long I have stretched forth my hands unto a disobedient and gainsaying people."

kids.question: Has God completely rejected Israel?
kids.option_a: No, God has not cast away His people — Paul himself is an Israelite as proof
kids.explanation: Romans 11:1 — "I say then, Hath God cast away his people? God forbid. For I also am an Israelite, of the seed of Abraham, of the tribe of Benjamin."

kids.question: What picture does Paul use about an olive tree?
kids.option_a: Wild branches (Gentiles) were grafted into the olive tree, and natural branches (Israel) were broken off
kids.option_b: The tree was chopped down completely
kids.option_c: Only Jewish branches grow on the tree
kids.explanation: Romans 11:17 — "And if some of the branches be broken off, and thou, being a wild olive tree, wert graffed in among them."

kids.question: What does Paul say about how deep God's wisdom is?
kids.option_a: It is so deep that no one can fully understand it
kids.option_b: It is easy for everyone to understand
kids.explanation: Romans 11:33 — "O the depth of the riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God! how unsearchable are his judgments, and his ways past finding out!"

teens.question: What Old Testament example does Paul give of a faithful remnant?
teens.option_a: In Elijah's time, God reserved 7,000 who had not bowed the knee to Baal — even so, there is a remnant according to the election of grace
teens.option_c: The Israelites crossing the Red Sea
teens.explanation: Romans 11:4-5 — "I have reserved to myself seven thousand men, who have not bowed the knee to the image of Baal. Even so then at this present time also there is a remnant according to the election of grace."

teens.question: What good came from Israel's stumbling?
teens.option_a: Salvation came to the Gentiles, to provoke Israel to jealousy
teens.explanation: Romans 11:11 — "Through their fall salvation is come unto the Gentiles, for to provoke them to jealousy."

teens.question: What warning does Paul give to Gentile believers?
teens.option_a: Do not be arrogant — if God did not spare the natural branches (Israel), He will not spare you either
teens.option_b: Gentiles are permanently safe no matter what
teens.explanation: Romans 11:21 — "For if God spared not the natural branches, take heed lest he also spare not thee."

teens.question: What does Paul say will eventually happen to Israel?
teens.option_a: All Israel shall be saved — the Deliverer shall come out of Sion and turn away ungodliness from Jacob
teens.option_d: Israel no longer matters in God's plan
teens.explanation: Romans 11:26 — "And so all Israel shall be saved: as it is written, There shall come out of Sion the Deliverer, and shall turn away ungodliness from Jacob."

teens.question: What does Paul say about God's gifts and calling?
teens.option_a: The gifts and calling of God are without repentance (irrevocable)
teens.option_b: God takes back His gifts when people disobey
teens.explanation: Romans 11:29 — "For the gifts and calling of God are without repentance."

adults.question: What does Paul's own conversion prove about God's plan for Israel?
adults.option_a: As a Benjamite and Israelite, Paul himself is proof that God has not entirely rejected Israel — a remnant exists by grace
adults.option_b: Paul's conversion is unrelated to Israel
adults.option_c: Paul ceased being an Israelite at conversion
adults.option_d: God replaced Israel with Paul alone
adults.explanation: Romans 11:1 — "Hath God cast away his people? God forbid. For I also am an Israelite, of the seed of Abraham, of the tribe of Benjamin."

adults.question: What does "election of grace" versus "works" mean in verses 5-6?
adults.option_a: The remnant is chosen by God's unmerited favour, not by their own merit — if by grace, then it is no more of works, otherwise grace is no more grace
adults.option_b: Grace and works can be combined
adults.option_d: Grace is a reward for effort
adults.explanation: Romans 11:5-6 — "There is a remnant according to the election of grace. And if by grace, then is it no more of works: otherwise grace is no more grace."

adults.question: What does the olive tree metaphor teach about the relationship between Israel and the church?
adults.option_a: The church is grafted into Israel's covenantal heritage (the root), not a replacement — Gentile believers partake of the root and fatness of the olive tree
adults.option_c: Israel and the church are completely separate
adults.option_d: The olive tree represents the Roman Empire
adults.explanation: Romans 11:17-18 — "Thou, being a wild olive tree, wert graffed in among them, and with them partakest of the root and fatness of the olive tree; Boast not against the branches."

adults.question: What is the "mystery" Paul reveals in verse 25?
adults.option_a: Blindness in part has happened to Israel until the fulness of the Gentiles be come in — then all Israel shall be saved
adults.option_b: The mystery is that Israel was never chosen
adults.option_c: The mystery is that Gentiles will eventually be rejected
adults.option_d: The mystery has never been revealed
adults.explanation: Romans 11:25-26 — "Blindness in part is happened to Israel, until the fulness of the Gentiles be come in. And so all Israel shall be saved."

adults.question: What Isaiah and Jeremiah prophecies does Paul cite regarding Israel's future salvation?
adults.option_a: Isaiah 59:20-21 and Isaiah 27:9 (with Jeremiah 31:33-34) — "The Deliverer shall come out of Sion... this is my covenant unto them, when I shall take away their sins"
adults.explanation: Romans 11:26-27 — "There shall come out of Sion the Deliverer, and shall turn away ungodliness from Jacob: For this is my covenant unto them, when I shall take away their sins."

adults.question: How does Paul explain the paradox of Israel being both enemies and beloved?
adults.option_a: As concerning the gospel, they are enemies for the Gentiles' sake; but as touching the election, they are beloved for the fathers' sakes — God's covenant faithfulness endures
adults.option_b: Israel can only be one or the other
adults.explanation: Romans 11:28 — "As concerning the gospel, they are enemies for your sakes: but as touching the election, they are beloved for the fathers' sakes."

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "God hath concluded them all in unbelief"?
adults.option_a: God has shut up all people — both Jews and Gentiles — under disobedience so that He might have mercy upon all, demonstrating that salvation is entirely by grace
adults.option_b: God wants everyone to remain in unbelief
adults.explanation: Romans 11:32 — "For God hath concluded them all in unbelief, that he might have mercy upon all."

adults.question: What is the significance of Paul's doxology in verses 33-36?
adults.option_a: It expresses overwhelming awe at God's wisdom in orchestrating salvation history — His judgments are unsearchable, His ways past finding out, and all things are from, through, and to Him
adults.option_b: It is a routine closing greeting
adults.option_d: It is addressed to the Roman emperor
adults.explanation: Romans 11:33,36 — "O the depth of the riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God! how unsearchable are his judgments, and his ways past finding out!... For of him, and through him, and to him, are all things: to whom be glory for ever. Amen."

kids.question: What does Paul say we should give to God as a living sacrifice?
kids.option_a: Our bodies — present your bodies a living sacrifice
kids.explanation: Romans 12:1 — "I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that ye present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God."

kids.question: How should we treat people who are mean to us?
kids.option_a: Be kind to them — overcome evil with good
kids.explanation: Romans 12:21 — "Be not overcome of evil, but overcome evil with good."

kids.question: Should we think of ourselves as better than others?
kids.option_a: No, we should not think more highly of ourselves than we ought to think
kids.option_b: Yes, if we are very smart
kids.option_c: Yes, if we go to church
kids.option_d: Yes, if our parents are important
kids.explanation: Romans 12:3 — "For I say... to every man that is among you, not to think of himself more highly than he ought to think; but to think soberly."

teens.question: What should we not be conformed to?
teens.option_a: This world — be not conformed to this world, but be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind
teens.explanation: Romans 12:2 — "And be not conformed to this world: but be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind, that ye may prove what is that good, and acceptable, and perfect, will of God."

teens.question: What does Paul teach about spiritual gifts using the body analogy?
teens.option_a: As one body has many members with different functions, so believers have different gifts and should use them faithfully
teens.explanation: Romans 12:4-6 — "For as we have many members in one body, and all members have not the same office: So we, being many, are one body in Christ... Having then gifts differing according to the grace that is given to us."

teens.question: What should our love be like?
teens.option_a: Without dissimulation (hypocrisy) — genuine and sincere
teens.explanation: Romans 12:9 — "Let love be without dissimulation. Abhor that which is evil; cleave to that which is good."

teens.question: How should believers respond to persecution?
teens.option_a: Bless them which persecute you: bless, and curse not
teens.explanation: Romans 12:14 — "Bless them which persecute you: bless, and curse not."

teens.question: What does Paul say about taking revenge?
teens.option_a: Do not avenge yourselves — leave room for God's wrath, for vengeance is mine, saith the Lord
teens.option_d: Only take revenge on serious offences
teens.explanation: Romans 12:19 — "Dearly beloved, avenge not yourselves, but rather give place unto wrath: for it is written, Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord."

adults.question: Why does Paul say "by the mercies of God" before his ethical exhortation?
adults.option_a: Christian ethics flow from gospel grace — the mercies described in chapters 1-11 are the motivation for the obedience described in chapters 12-16
adults.option_b: It is merely a polite greeting
adults.option_c: He is quoting a Jewish prayer
adults.option_d: It has no connection to the preceding chapters
adults.explanation: Romans 12:1 — "I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God." The word "therefore" connects all of Paul's theology (ch.1-11) to the practical Christian life.

adults.question: What does "living sacrifice" mean in contrast to Old Testament sacrifices?
adults.option_a: Unlike OT animal sacrifices that died, believers offer their living bodies in ongoing, active devotion to God — a continuous act of worship in daily life
adults.option_c: It refers to animal sacrifice continuing
adults.option_d: It is a metaphor with no practical application
