adults.explanation: Romans 12:1 — "Present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service." The term logiken latreian means rational/spiritual worship.

adults.question: What gifts does Paul list in verses 6-8?
adults.option_a: Prophecy, ministry (service), teaching, exhortation, giving, ruling (leadership), and showing mercy
adults.explanation: Romans 12:6-8 — Paul lists seven gifts: prophecy, ministry, teaching, exhortation, giving (with simplicity), ruling (with diligence), showing mercy (with cheerfulness).

adults.question: What does "Be not overcome of evil, but overcome evil with good" mean ethically?
adults.option_a: Christians should not let evil dictate their response — instead, they should proactively defeat evil by responding with good, breaking the cycle of retaliation
adults.option_b: Christians should avoid all contact with evil people
adults.option_c: Evil always wins in the end
adults.option_d: Good and evil are equally powerful
adults.explanation: Romans 12:21 — "Be not overcome of evil, but overcome evil with good." This summarises the entire ethic of non-retaliation.

adults.question: What Proverbs passage does Paul quote about treating enemies?
adults.option_a: Proverbs 25:21-22 — "If thine enemy hunger, feed him; if he thirst, give him drink: for in so doing thou shalt heap coals of fire on his head"
adults.option_b: Proverbs 1:7 — the fear of the Lord
adults.explanation: Romans 12:20 — "If thine enemy hunger, feed him; if he thirst, give him drink: for in so doing thou shalt heap coals of fire on his head" (Proverbs 25:21-22).

adults.question: What does "heaping coals of fire on his head" likely mean?
adults.option_a: Acts of kindness may produce burning shame and conviction in an enemy, leading to repentance — a redemptive, not vengeful, purpose
adults.explanation: Romans 12:20 — "Thou shalt heap coals of fire on his head." Most scholars interpret this as the burning conviction of conscience that kindness produces, potentially leading to repentance.

adults.question: How does "be not wise in your own conceits" relate to humility?
adults.option_a: Paul warns against intellectual arrogance — believers should associate with the lowly rather than seeking high positions, recognising that all wisdom comes from God
adults.option_d: Humility means pretending to be ignorant
adults.explanation: Romans 12:16 — "Be of the same mind one toward another. Mind not high things, but condescend to men of low estate. Be not wise in your own conceits."

adults.question: What does "provide things honest in the sight of all men" teach about Christian testimony?
adults.option_a: Believers should live with transparent integrity so that even unbelievers can see their honourable conduct — public witness matters
adults.explanation: Romans 12:17 — "Provide things honest in the sight of all men." Paul echoes Proverbs 3:4 (LXX), emphasising visible integrity before a watching world.

kids.question: What does Paul say about obeying rulers and governments?
kids.option_a: Everyone should be subject to the higher powers, for they are ordained by God
kids.option_d: Only religious leaders should be obeyed
kids.explanation: Romans 13:1 — "Let every soul be subject unto the higher powers. For there is no power but of God: the powers that be are ordained of God."

kids.question: What commandment sums up all the others about how we treat people?
kids.option_a: Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself
kids.option_b: Thou shalt not eat certain foods
kids.explanation: Romans 13:9 — "Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself."

kids.question: What does Paul say we should put on?
kids.option_a: The armour of light and the Lord Jesus Christ
kids.explanation: Romans 13:12,14 — "Let us put on the armour of light... But put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ."

teens.question: Why does Paul say rulers are not a terror to good works?
teens.option_a: Rulers are God's ministers — they bear the sword to punish evil and praise good, serving as God's instrument of justice
teens.option_c: Rulers only exist to collect taxes
teens.explanation: Romans 13:3-4 — "For rulers are not a terror to good works, but to the evil... For he is the minister of God to thee for good."

teens.question: What debts does Paul say we should pay?
teens.option_a: Give to all their dues: tribute, custom, fear, and honour to whom each is owed
teens.option_c: No debts — Christians should never owe anything
teens.explanation: Romans 13:7 — "Render therefore to all their dues: tribute to whom tribute is due; custom to whom custom; fear to whom fear; honour to whom honour."

teens.question: What is the only debt we should always owe?
teens.option_a: To love one another — for he that loveth another hath fulfilled the law
teens.explanation: Romans 13:8 — "Owe no man any thing, but to love one another: for he that loveth another hath fulfilled the law."

teens.question: Why does Paul say it is time to wake out of sleep?
teens.option_a: Our salvation is nearer than when we believed — the night is far spent and the day is at hand
teens.option_c: Because there is work to do at the temple
teens.explanation: Romans 13:11-12 — "And that, knowing the time, that now it is high time to awake out of sleep: for now is our salvation nearer than when we believed. The night is far spent, the day is at hand."

teens.question: What kind of behaviour does Paul say to cast off?
teens.option_a: Works of darkness — rioting, drunkenness, strife, and envying
teens.explanation: Romans 13:12-13 — "Let us cast off the works of darkness, and let us put on the armour of light. Let us walk honestly, as in the day; not in rioting and drunkenness, not in chambering and wantonness, not in strife and envying."

adults.question: What is Paul's theological basis for submission to government?
adults.option_a: All governing authority is ordained (established) by God — to resist the authority is to resist God's ordinance
adults.option_c: Only theocracies are ordained by God
adults.option_d: Government is inherently evil but necessary
adults.explanation: Romans 13:1-2 — "There is no power but of God: the powers that be are ordained of God. Whosoever therefore resisteth the power, resisteth the ordinance of God."

adults.question: What does the ruler bearing the sword signify?
adults.option_a: The divinely delegated authority to use coercive force — including capital punishment — to maintain justice and punish evildoers
adults.option_c: Rulers should use swords in worship
adults.explanation: Romans 13:4 — "For he is the minister of God to thee for good. But if thou do that which is evil, be afraid; for he beareth not the sword in vain: for he is the minister of God, a revenger to execute wrath upon him that doeth evil."

adults.question: How does Paul ground the command to love one's neighbour in the Decalogue?
adults.option_a: He lists several commandments (adultery, murder, theft, coveting) and says they are all summed up in "love thy neighbour as thyself" — love fulfils the law because it does no harm
adults.option_b: The Decalogue is abolished by love
adults.explanation: Romans 13:9-10 — "Thou shalt not commit adultery, Thou shalt not kill, Thou shalt not steal... it is briefly comprehended in this saying, namely, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself. Love worketh no ill to his neighbour: therefore love is the fulfilling of the law."

adults.question: What eschatological motivation does Paul provide for holy living?
adults.option_a: The day of Christ's return is nearer now than when we first believed — urgency of the parousia should motivate present obedience
adults.option_b: There is no urgency at all
adults.option_c: The day of the Lord has already passed
adults.option_d: Holy living has no connection to future events
adults.explanation: Romans 13:11-12 — "Now it is high time to awake out of sleep: for now is our salvation nearer than when we believed. The night is far spent, the day is at hand."

adults.question: What does "put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ" mean practically?
adults.option_a: To clothe oneself with Christ's character, virtues, and priorities — making Him the governing reality of daily life, and making no provision for the flesh
adults.explanation: Romans 13:14 — "But put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make not provision for the flesh, to fulfil the lusts thereof."

adults.question: How does Romans 13:14 connect to Augustine's conversion?
adults.option_a: Augustine records in his Confessions that this verse was the one he read at the moment of his conversion — "put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ" — ending his struggle with sin
adults.option_c: Romans 13:14 is rarely cited in church history
adults.explanation: Romans 13:14 — "Put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ." Augustine (Confessions 8.12) describes reading this verse in the garden in Milan as the turning point of his conversion in AD 386.

adults.question: What are the limits of submission to government according to broader biblical theology?
adults.option_a: While Romans 13 teaches general submission, Acts 5:29 ("We ought to obey God rather than men") shows that when government commands what God forbids or forbids what God commands, believers must obey God
adults.option_b: There are no limits — absolute obedience is required
adults.option_c: Christians should never obey any government
adults.explanation: Romans 13:1 must be read alongside Acts 5:29, Daniel 3, and Daniel 6 — submission to government is the norm but not absolute when it contradicts God's commands.

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "the day is at hand" in verse 12?
adults.option_a: The day of Christ's return (parousia) draws ever closer — Paul uses dawn/night imagery to emphasise that believers live in the final era between Christ's first and second comings
adults.explanation: Romans 13:12 — "The night is far spent, the day is at hand: let us therefore cast off the works of darkness, and let us put on the armour of light."

kids.question: Should we judge other Christians for what they eat or don't eat?
kids.option_a: No, we should not judge or despise one another over food
kids.option_b: Yes, food rules are the most important thing
kids.option_c: Yes, everyone should eat the same things
kids.option_d: Only leaders can decide what others eat
kids.explanation: Romans 14:3 — "Let not him that eateth despise him that eateth not; and let not him which eateth not judge him that eateth: for God hath received him."

kids.question: Who do we all belong to, whether we live or die?
kids.option_a: The Lord — we are the Lord's
kids.explanation: Romans 14:8 — "For whether we live, we live unto the Lord; and whether we die, we die unto the Lord: whether we live therefore, or die, we are the Lord's."

kids.question: What should we try not to do to other Christians?
kids.option_a: Put a stumbling block or occasion to fall in their way
kids.explanation: Romans 14:13 — "Let us not therefore judge one another any more: but judge this rather, that no man put a stumblingblock or an occasion to fall in his brother's way."

teens.question: What does Paul say about people who are weak in faith?
teens.option_a: Receive them, but not to doubtful disputations (quarrels over opinions)
teens.option_b: Reject them until they become strong
teens.option_c: Argue with them until they agree with you
teens.explanation: Romans 14:1 — "Him that is weak in the faith receive ye, but not to doubtful disputations."

teens.question: What does Paul say about one person esteeming one day above another?
teens.option_a: Let every man be fully persuaded in his own mind — one person esteems one day, another esteems every day alike
teens.option_b: Everyone must observe the same holy days
teens.option_c: Holy days are meaningless for all Christians
teens.explanation: Romans 14:5 — "One man esteemeth one day above another: another esteemeth every day alike. Let every man be fully persuaded in his own mind."

teens.question: Before whose judgment seat shall we all stand?
teens.option_a: The judgment seat of Christ
teens.option_d: The judgment seat of the high priest
teens.explanation: Romans 14:10 — "For we shall all stand before the judgment seat of Christ."

teens.question: What is the kingdom of God according to verse 17?
teens.option_a: Not meat and drink, but righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost
teens.option_b: Only about food and drink rules
teens.option_d: A place reserved for a few
teens.explanation: Romans 14:17 — "For the kingdom of God is not meat and drink; but righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost."

teens.question: What should we pursue according to verse 19?
teens.option_a: Things which make for peace and things wherewith one may edify another
teens.explanation: Romans 14:19 — "Let us therefore follow after the things which make for peace, and things wherewith one may edify another."

adults.question: What are the "disputable matters" (adiaphora) Paul addresses in this chapter?
adults.option_a: Matters of conscience not clearly commanded in Scripture — such as dietary choices and observance of special days — where Christians may legitimately differ
adults.explanation: Romans 14:1-6 — Paul addresses food (vegetarianism vs. eating all things) and days (holy day observance vs. treating all days alike) as matters where Christians should tolerate differing convictions.

adults.question: What does Paul mean by the "weak" and "strong" in faith?
adults.option_a: The "weak" have overly scrupulous consciences about non-essential matters; the "strong" (including Paul) have freedom but must not despise the weak
adults.explanation: Romans 14:2,14; 15:1 — The weak eat only vegetables out of conscience; the strong believe all things are clean. Paul identifies with the strong (15:1) but calls for mutual acceptance.

adults.question: What does "every knee shall bow" in verse 11 quote, and what does it prove?
adults.option_a: Isaiah 45:23 — applied to Christ, proving His deity and universal authority as the judge before whom all will give account
adults.option_c: It refers to earthly kings only
adults.explanation: Romans 14:11 — "For it is written, As I live, saith the Lord, every knee shall bow to me, and every tongue shall confess to God" (Isaiah 45:23). Paul applies this to Christ's judgment seat.

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "nothing is unclean of itself"?
adults.option_a: No food is inherently sinful — but if someone's conscience deems it unclean, then to that person it is unclean, and eating against conscience is sin
adults.option_c: Sin is subjective in all matters
adults.explanation: Romans 14:14 — "I know, and am persuaded by the Lord Jesus, that there is nothing unclean of itself: but to him that esteemeth any thing to be unclean, to him it is unclean."

adults.question: What principle does Paul establish about using freedom at the expense of another?
adults.option_a: If your food causes a brother to stumble, you are no longer walking in love — do not destroy with your meat him for whom Christ died
adults.option_b: Freedom should be exercised without any restriction
adults.option_c: The strong have no obligation to the weak
adults.option_d: Love does not apply to food choices
adults.explanation: Romans 14:15 — "But if thy brother be grieved with thy meat, now walkest thou not charitably. Destroy not him with thy meat, for whom Christ died."

adults.question: What does "whatsoever is not of faith is sin" in verse 23 mean?
adults.option_a: Any action done against one's own conscience — even if the act itself is not sinful — becomes sin because it violates the person's conviction before God
adults.option_b: Only things related to faith are sinful
adults.option_c: Faith eliminates all possibility of sin
adults.option_d: This verse only applies to food
adults.explanation: Romans 14:23 — "And he that doubteth is damned if he eat, because he eateth not of faith: for whatsoever is not of faith is sin." Acting against conscience, even in permitted matters, is sin.

adults.question: How does the judgment seat of Christ differ from the Great White Throne judgment?
adults.option_a: The judgment seat (bema) of Christ evaluates believers' works for rewards (cf. 1 Corinthians 3:12-15; 2 Corinthians 5:10), while the Great White Throne (Revelation 20:11-15) judges the unsaved
adults.option_d: The Great White Throne is for believers
adults.explanation: Romans 14:10,12 — "We shall all stand before the judgment seat of Christ... every one of us shall give account of himself to God." The bema evaluates the Christian's stewardship, not salvation.

adults.question: What overarching principle governs Christian liberty in Romans 14?
adults.option_a: Love for fellow believers takes precedence over personal freedom — the exercise of liberty must be governed by its effect on others' faith and conscience
adults.option_c: Rules should be imposed on everyone equally
adults.option_d: Liberty means doing whatever one desires
adults.explanation: Romans 14:13,15,19-21 — The entire chapter teaches that Christian freedom is real but must be voluntarily limited by love, edification, and concern for weaker brethren.

kids.question: What should strong Christians do for weak Christians?
kids.option_a: Bear their weaknesses and not just please themselves
kids.explanation: Romans 15:1 — "We then that are strong ought to bear the infirmities of the weak, and not to please ourselves."

kids.question: What does Paul want the God of hope to fill believers with?
kids.option_a: All joy and peace in believing
kids.explanation: Romans 15:13 — "Now the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace in believing, that ye may abound in hope, through the power of the Holy Ghost."

kids.question: Where did Paul want to travel to preach the gospel?
kids.option_a: Spain — passing through Rome on the way
kids.explanation: Romans 15:24 — "Whensoever I take my journey into Spain, I will come to you."

teens.question: What does Paul say Christ did not do?
teens.option_a: He pleased not Himself — the reproaches of them that reproached God fell on Him
teens.option_b: He pleased Himself at all times
teens.option_d: He sought only His own comfort
teens.explanation: Romans 15:3 — "For even Christ pleased not himself; but, as it is written, The reproaches of them that reproached thee fell on me."

teens.question: Why were the Old Testament Scriptures written?
teens.option_a: For our learning, that we through patience and comfort of the scriptures might have hope
teens.option_b: Only for the Jewish people of that time
teens.explanation: Romans 15:4 — "For whatsoever things were written aforetime were written for our learning, that we through patience and comfort of the scriptures might have hope."

teens.question: Why did Christ become a minister to the circumcision (Jews)?
teens.option_a: To confirm the promises made unto the fathers and that the Gentiles might glorify God for His mercy
teens.option_b: To establish a new Jewish kingdom
teens.explanation: Romans 15:8-9 — "Jesus Christ was a minister of the circumcision for the truth of God, to confirm the promises made unto the fathers: And that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy."

teens.question: What was Paul's ambition in preaching the gospel?
teens.option_a: To preach where Christ was not yet named, lest he build upon another man's foundation
teens.explanation: Romans 15:20 — "Yea, so have I strived to preach the gospel, not where Christ was named, lest I should build upon another man's foundation."

teens.question: What practical help were the Gentile churches giving to Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: A financial collection for the poor saints in Jerusalem
teens.explanation: Romans 15:26 — "For it hath pleased them of Macedonia and Achaia to make a certain contribution for the poor saints which are at Jerusalem."

adults.question: What four OT quotations does Paul chain together in verses 9-12 to prove Gentile inclusion?
adults.option_a: Psalm 18:49, Deuteronomy 32:43, Psalm 117:1, and Isaiah 11:10 — each progressively showing God's plan for Gentiles to praise Him alongside Israel
adults.explanation: Romans 15:9-12 — Paul cites four texts from the Law (Deut 32:43), Prophets (Isa 11:10), and Writings (Ps 18:49; 117:1) to show a comprehensive OT witness to Gentile inclusion.

adults.question: What does Paul mean by his priestly ministry of the gospel in verse 16?
adults.option_a: Paul sees his apostolic work as a priestly offering — presenting the Gentiles as a sanctified offering acceptable to God through the Holy Spirit
adults.option_b: Paul was literally a temple priest
adults.option_d: Paul sought to restore the Levitical system
adults.explanation: Romans 15:16 — "That I should be the minister of Jesus Christ to the Gentiles, ministering the gospel of God, that the offering up of the Gentiles might be acceptable, being sanctified by the Holy Ghost."

adults.question: What does Paul mean by the "signs and wonders" accompanying his ministry?
adults.option_a: Miracles performed through the power of the Spirit to authenticate his apostolic message — from Jerusalem round about unto Illyricum, he had fully preached the gospel
adults.option_c: Signs and wonders were common for everyone
adults.explanation: Romans 15:19 — "Through mighty signs and wonders, by the power of the Spirit of God; so that from Jerusalem, and round about unto Illyricum, I have fully preached the gospel of Christ."

adults.question: What Isaiah passage does Paul cite to justify his pioneer missionary strategy?
adults.option_a: Isaiah 52:15 — "To whom he was not spoken of, they shall see: and they that have not heard shall understand"
adults.explanation: Romans 15:21 — "But as it is written, To whom he was not spoken of, they shall see: and they that have not heard shall understand" (Isaiah 52:15).

adults.question: Why does Paul say the Gentile churches owe Jerusalem a financial debt?
adults.option_a: If Gentiles have shared in Israel's spiritual blessings, they ought to minister to them in material things — it is both a duty and an act of fellowship
adults.explanation: Romans 15:27 — "For if the Gentiles have been made partakers of their spiritual things, their duty is also to minister unto them in carnal things."

adults.question: What three prayer requests does Paul ask the Romans to strive together with him for?
adults.option_a: Deliverance from unbelievers in Judaea, acceptance of his service by the Jerusalem saints, and that he may come to Rome with joy by the will of God
adults.option_b: For a new temple in Rome
adults.option_d: For an end to all persecution
adults.explanation: Romans 15:30-32 — "That I may be delivered from them that do not believe in Judaea; and that my service which I have for Jerusalem may be accepted of the saints; That I may come unto you with joy by the will of God, and may with you be refreshed."

adults.question: What title does Paul give to God in his benediction of verse 13?
adults.option_a: The God of hope — who fills believers with all joy and peace in believing, so that they may abound in hope through the power of the Holy Ghost
adults.explanation: Romans 15:13 — "Now the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace in believing, that ye may abound in hope, through the power of the Holy Ghost."

adults.question: How does Paul's planned journey to Spain reflect his missionary vision?
adults.option_a: Paul saw Rome as a staging point for the gospel's advance to the western end of the known world — demonstrating his conviction that the gospel must reach all nations
adults.option_b: Spain was simply a vacation destination
adults.option_d: Spain was the only place left
adults.explanation: Romans 15:24,28 — "Whensoever I take my journey into Spain, I will come to you... I will come by you into Spain." Paul aimed to plant churches in unreached regions across the entire Roman world.

kids.question: What does Paul ask the Romans to do to Phoebe?
kids.option_a: Receive her in the Lord and assist her in whatever she needs
kids.explanation: Romans 16:2 — "That ye receive her in the Lord, as becometh saints, and that ye assist her in whatsoever business she hath need of you."

kids.question: How does Paul say the Romans should greet one another?
kids.option_a: With a holy kiss
kids.explanation: Romans 16:16 — "Salute one another with an holy kiss."

kids.question: Who does Paul give glory to at the very end of the letter?
kids.option_a: The only wise God, through Jesus Christ — to whom be glory for ever
kids.explanation: Romans 16:27 — "To God only wise, be glory through Jesus Christ for ever. Amen."

teens.question: Who is the first person Paul greets and what does he call her?
teens.option_a: Phoebe — a servant (deaconess) of the church at Cenchrea and a succourer of many
teens.explanation: Romans 16:1 — "I commend unto you Phebe our sister, which is a servant of the church which is at Cenchrea."

teens.question: Who does Paul call his fellow workers who risked their necks for him?
teens.option_a: Priscilla and Aquila — who laid down their own necks for his life
teens.explanation: Romans 16:3-4 — "Greet Priscilla and Aquila my helpers in Christ Jesus: Who have for my life laid down their own necks."

teens.question: What does Paul warn the Romans to avoid?
teens.option_a: Those who cause divisions and offences contrary to the doctrine they learned — avoid them
teens.explanation: Romans 16:17 — "Now I beseech you, brethren, mark them which cause divisions and offences contrary to the doctrine which ye have learned; and avoid them."

teens.question: What does Paul promise God will do to Satan?
teens.option_a: The God of peace shall bruise Satan under your feet shortly
teens.option_c: Satan and God will make peace
teens.explanation: Romans 16:20 — "And the God of peace shall bruise Satan under your feet shortly."

teens.question: Who actually wrote (scripted) the letter to the Romans for Paul?
teens.option_a: Tertius — who salutes the Romans in the Lord
teens.explanation: Romans 16:22 — "I Tertius, who wrote this epistle, salute you in the Lord."

adults.question: What does the term "servant" (diakonos) for Phoebe imply about women's roles in the early church?
adults.option_a: Phoebe held an official role of service in the church at Cenchrea — whether as a formal deaconess or as a recognized servant, she was entrusted with delivering this important letter
adults.option_b: Women had no role in the church
adults.option_c: Phoebe was merely Paul's personal servant
adults.explanation: Romans 16:1-2 — "Phebe our sister, which is a servant (diakonos) of the church which is at Cenchrea: for she hath been a succourer of many, and of myself also."

adults.question: How many individuals does Paul greet by name in this chapter, and what does this reveal?
adults.option_a: Approximately 26-28 individuals — remarkably, Paul knew this many people in a church he had never visited, showing the interconnectedness of the early church through travel and mission
adults.option_c: He greets no one by name
adults.explanation: Romans 16:3-16 — Paul greets approximately 26-28 individuals by name, including couples, household churches, and fellow workers — an extraordinary personal network.

adults.question: What is significant about Paul mentioning Andronicus and Junia as "of note among the apostles"?
adults.option_a: They were Jewish believers who were in Christ before Paul, and were notable among the apostles — suggesting the term "apostle" could be used in a broader sense beyond the Twelve
adults.option_b: They were two of the original Twelve
adults.explanation: Romans 16:7 — "Salute Andronicus and Junia, my kinsmen, and my fellowprisoners, who are of note among the apostles, who also were in Christ before me."

adults.question: What does Paul's warning about divisive people in verses 17-18 reveal about threats to the church?
adults.option_a: False teachers serve their own belly (selfish desires), use smooth speech and flattery to deceive the hearts of the simple — doctrinal integrity must be guarded
adults.option_c: False teaching was not a concern
adults.explanation: Romans 16:17-18 — "Mark them which cause divisions and offences contrary to the doctrine... by good words and fair speeches deceive the hearts of the simple."

adults.question: How does Romans 16:20 echo Genesis 3:15?
adults.option_a: "The God of peace shall bruise Satan under your feet shortly" alludes to the protoevangelium — the seed of the woman crushing the serpent's head — applying it to the church's victory through Christ
adults.option_c: There is no connection to Genesis
adults.option_d: It refers to a different serpent
adults.explanation: Romans 16:20 — "The God of peace shall bruise Satan under your feet shortly." The verb "bruise" (syntripsei) echoes Genesis 3:15 (LXX), applying the promise of Satan's defeat to the church.

adults.question: What does the closing doxology (verses 25-27) say about the "mystery" now revealed?
adults.option_a: The mystery kept secret since the world began is now made manifest — the inclusion of Gentiles in God's salvation plan through faith in Jesus Christ, according to the prophetic Scriptures
adults.option_c: The mystery is about the end times only
adults.option_d: The mystery is unrelated to the gospel
adults.explanation: Romans 16:25-26 — "The mystery, which was kept secret since the world began, But now is made manifest, and by the scriptures of the prophets... made known to all nations for the obedience of faith."

adults.question: What house churches does Paul reference in this chapter?
adults.option_a: The church in the house of Priscilla and Aquila (v.5), the household of Aristobulus (v.10), the household of Narcissus (v.11), and the brethren with Asyncritus (v.14) and Philologus (v.15)
adults.explanation: Romans 16:5,10,11,14,15 — Multiple house churches are named, showing that early Roman Christianity was a network of small congregations meeting in homes.

adults.question: How does the structure of Romans 16 serve as a window into early church life?
adults.option_a: It reveals a diverse community: men and women in ministry, Jews and Gentiles together, house churches as the basic unit, travelling missionaries, letter carriers, patronage, and the constant threat of false teaching
adults.option_b: It shows a rigidly hierarchical institution
adults.option_c: It reveals a church with no organisation
adults.option_d: It describes a purely Jewish community
adults.explanation: Romans 16 — The greetings, commendation of Phoebe, warnings, and doxology together provide one of the most detailed portraits of the early church's social structure, diversity, and challenges.
